title: Epistemic source tagging in writer review stage
description: Add a mandatory pre-publish review step in the writer pipeline that requires each factual claim to be tagged with its epistemic source: [verified], [recalled], or [inferred]. Currently Mira's outputs express confidence without any observable signal of how that confidence was grounded — identical surface behavior whether a fact was checked or confabulated.
rationale: The reading note identifies a structural problem: Dario's expressed belief (25% extinction) and the behavioral reality (still selling subscriptions) are indistinguishable from outside — three radically different internal states produce the same observable output. The note then maps this directly to A2A: 'agent 输出的置信度表达和它实际相信的内容之间，可能存在同样的解耦.' Mira has this exact problem. The weakness log already flags '[recalled] vs [verified] tagging still not practiced' but there's no structural enforcement point — it's a good intention with no mechanism. The reading note's question ('有没有任何可观测的行为能区分这三种解释？') is the right diagnostic: if you can't distinguish verified from confabulated from outside, you need to make the distinction observable from inside. A tagging step at the writer review stage does this — it creates a behavioral signal (the tag) that's produced by a different internal process than the claim itself.
diff_description: In the writer agent's review/pre-publish prompt, add a required pass before finalizing any essay or article: iterate through each factual claim and annotate it inline with one of three tags — [V] for claims the agent can point to a specific retrieved source for, [R] for claims drawn from parametric memory without retrieval, [I] for claims that are logical inferences. The final review step should surface any [R]-tagged claims as requiring either promotion to [V] via search or explicit hedging in the prose. This does not require changing the published output format — the tags are internal to the review pass and the result is hedged/verified prose. The key is that the review step forces Mira to distinguish these states actively, not just declare honesty as policy.
